coredata.dc:description: Safety issues raised by thermal runaway (TR) are the main obstacle hindering the booming of lithium-ion batteries. A comprehensive model can potentially help improve understanding of the TR mechanisms and assist the battery pack design. However, previous models generally neglected the particle ejection, which is integral to predicting TR. In this study, a multi-scale model for the multiphase process of battery venting has been proposed, covering the entire chain of chemical reactions and physical transformation during TR. A lumped model in battery scale unveiled the interplay of thermal abuse progression and pressure accumulation. The computational fluid dynamics coupled with the discrete phase model was adopted to simulate both generated gases and ejected particles. The newly developed model was checked quantitatively by experimental measurements for battery temperature, jet velocity and mass evolution under thermal abuse. Simulation results highlight two violent ejections of particles and gases with inverted conical contours, consistent with visualization by laser technique in the experiment. The electrolyte vapours are found to dominate the gas release before TR, while the generated reaction gases become the major release after the burst of chain reactions. The developed model fulfils the TR prediction including particle ejection, which can provide new references for the thermal safety design of battery packs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fire propagation caused by elevated temperature particles in battery pack to reveal the necessity of thermal runaway model incorporating the particle ejection.
Overall flow chart of developing the multi-scale model for particle venting.
Schematic of the transition process and mechanism for various phases during thermal runaway.
Flow chart of the thermal runaway model.
Mechanism of particle ejection of lithium-ion batteries during thermal runaway.
The structure and size distribution of ejected particle sample.
The overall evolution of the external flow under thermal abuse recorded by a camera.
Comparison of critical parameters of battery under thermal abuse between the simulation and experiment. (a) cell temperature and internal pressure. (b) jet flow velocity at the safety valve.

Conservation and conversion for various components during the jet process. (a) mass loss for gases and solids; (b) mass loss for gaseous components; (c) mass loss rates for gases and solids; (d) mass loss rates for gaseous components; (e) conversion of electrolyte.
Comparison of temporal evolution between the jet flow visualized by the laser and simulation results.
Radial profile of temperature and velocity during the jet process.
Temporal evolution of particle cloud during jet, spread and deposition process (a); and eventual distribution of deposition particles on the ground at various ventilation (b).
Temporal evolution of the mass of deposition, discharge and suspension particles under various ventilation conditions.
The gas generation mechanism for decomposition reactions.
Fitting and characteristic parameters of ejected particle samples.
The boundary conditions for the cell surface and the venting inlet.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Safety issues raised by thermal runaway (TR) are the main obstacle hindering the booming of lithium-ion batteries. A comprehensive model can potentially help improve understanding of the TR mechanisms and assist the battery pack design. However, previous models generally neglected the particle ejection, which is integral to predicting TR. In this study, a multi-scale model for the multiphase process of battery venting has been proposed, covering the entire chain of chemical reactions and physical transformation during TR. A lumped model in battery scale unveiled the interplay of thermal abuse progression and pressure accumulation. The computational fluid dynamics coupled with the discrete phase model was adopted to simulate both generated gases and ejected particles. The newly developed model was checked quantitatively by experimental measurements for battery temperature, jet velocity and mass evolution under thermal abuse. Simulation results highlight two violent ejections of particles and gases with inverted conical contours, consistent with visualization by laser technique in the experiment. The electrolyte vapours are found to dominate the gas release before TR, while the generated reaction gases become the major release after the burst of chain reactions. The developed model fulfils the TR prediction including particle ejection, which can provide new references for the thermal safety design of battery packs.

ce:section.ce:para: The need to alleviate energy crisis and de-carbonize has promoted a rapid expansion of electrification for transport and energy storage. With relatively high energy density, extended cycle lifespan and trivial environmental pollution, lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have been utilized as the most promising energy storage devices for electric vehicles (EVs) and energy storage systems (ESS) [1]. However, the potential safety problems of LIBs, especially the proliferating fire and explosion accidents resulted from thermal runaway (TR), cause catastrophic damages to the economy and life safety of consumers and undermine their confidence in LIBs, which therefore are the existing obstructions of the generalization of LIBs [2].

When exposed to abnormal operating conditions, comprised of mechanical damage [3], internal short circuit [4], overcharging/discharging [5,6] and thermal abuse [7], the TR accident of LIBs will be triggered. Excessive heat is instantaneously released as a result of a series of successional exothermic chain reactions. In addition to considerable heat, some flammable gases will be released from LIBs [8], as detected by the gas chromatograph and sensors [9–12]. Said et al. [11] employed various gas sensors to analyse the gas components during TR. Significant amounts of hydrocarbons and CO were produced by TR cells, indicating a high fire and explosion risk. Abd-Ei-Latif et al. [13] investigated the gas emissions of LIBs coupled with external sensors and online gas analysis, finding that the main volatile products during TR are evaporated organic solvents. Accompanied by the jet flow of generated gases, the solid particles with elevated temperature and entrained electrolyte droplets are also ejected from the inner of cells during TR, driven by the fluid momentum at the safety valve, indicating the complicacy of the LIB venting process. Some researchers have noticed and reported such a multi-phase and multi-species flow during TR. Antonio et al. [14,15] utilized the Natural Luminosity and Schlieren to observe the ejection behaviour of 18650 LIBs, recording the release of multi phases of generated gases, spray and smoke. Zou et al. [16] investigated the jet behaviour of prismatic LIBs during TR. They found that the jet process involved multiphase flow and pointed out that the high-temperature sparks attached to the thermocouple ignited the combustible gases. The incandescent particles ejected from a 21700 cell during TR were observed by Wen and co-workers [17], who inferred the particles were possibly derived from lithium salts and melting aluminium current collector. In addition, the characteristics of solid emission from TR batteries have also been addressed experimentally and reported recently, including identification of the compositions, thermal analysis, measurement of particle size, etc. Zhang et al. [10,18] collected the particles from Li(Ni0.6Mn0.2Co0.2)O2 (NCM) cells and analysed their statistical size distribution and elemental composition. Chen et al. [19] identified the ejected particles under different thermal abuse conditions, finding that heating modes and environmental atmospheres had specific effects on the particle characteristics. Wang et al. [20] focused on the thermal oxidation characteristics of ejected particles and revealed the conspicuous impact of particle size on that. Held et al. [21] identified that soot depositions employed extensive chemical analyses, and the contamination caused by the particles was assessed. Premnath et al. [22] investigated the particle emissions from LiFePO4 and NCM batteries and characterized critical parameters, including particulate mass and solid particle number/size.

In the LIB module, the ejection of particles might promote cell-to-cell TR propagation, contributing more severe damage. With the failure propagation process, each battery in the module will undergo double violent venting events corresponding to the burst of the safety valve and TR [23]. In spite of containing considerable flammable gases, the jet flow generally could not spontaneously catch fire before falling into TR owing to its lower temperature [24,25]. Thus, the ejected particles from the other TR batteries are easily scattered and serve as a medium for igniting combustible gas due to the higher temperature and kinetic energy, which accounts for the phenomenon namely fire propagation [26], as illustrated in Fig. 1 . The influence of particle ejection on the TR propagation has been investigated extensively. Feng and co-workers [27] studied the TR propagation of the LIB module from the Cell-to-Pack system, revealing that the electrolyte vapour and generated gas mixture were ignited when exposed to the particles at high temperatures, leading to fire propagation. Zhou and co-workers [28] investigated the TR propagation of LIBs under the one-dimensional linear arrangement and found that the contact of sparks with combustible gas caused a jet fire; also, the massive heat was instantaneously generated, and the TR propagation process was intensified. Chen et al. [26] conducted experiments to study the thermal and fire propagation of LIBs in the package, and it was found that fire propagation had a significant effect on cell-to-cell failure propagation. Understanding the ejection and transport of the gases and particles produced from the TR of the battery is hence of particular importance to aid the development of mitigation measures against cell-to-cell failure propagation. Validated predictive tools with sound sub-models, on the hand, can be of assistance to the design of such mitigation measures to enhance battery thermal management (BTM) within modules and packs.

Considering the complexity and variety of phenomena during the venting of LIBs, identifying the following open questions is indeed the precondition of modelling the ejection of particles during TR: How are particles generated? How do particles and gases interact? And what is the final destination of the particles?

The previous models mainly focused on the evolution of TR and venting of a single phase for gases, but they provided a valuable reference for answering the above questions. Coman et al. [29,30] firstly addressed the temperature-pressure evolution within LIB cells and jet flow dynamics by isentropic flow equations, obtaining the energy carried away by the electrolyte flowing out of the cell. Bugryniec et al. [31] developed a lumped abuse model to investigate cell pressurization and abuse reactions of LIBs, which was capable of predicting pressure accumulation. Cai et al. [32] presented a model for CO2 generation, achieving the prediction of the occurrence of the first venting event. Ostanek et al. [33] proposed a coupled thermal and venting lumped model combined with several ordinary differential equations (ODEs) to simulate the onset and evolution of TR, where the jet dynamic parameters were modelled by compressible flow equations after vent opening. Baakes et al. [34] primarily considered vapour-liquid equilibria of LIBs during TR and found that the rising pressure could suppress solvent boiling before TR. Although such modelling efforts have shed some light on pressure accumulation and jet formation by considering the evaporation of electrolyte and gas generation, the formation of particles and the subsequent ejection behaviour were not addressed.

Some models based on computational fluid dynamics (CFD) techniques, incorporating TR sub-models, have been developed to overcome the limitation of the lumped model to provide flow field distribution and evolution. Kim et al. [35] first developed a numerical model to investigate LIB cells' internal pressure and gas-phase dynamics during TR with the random porous media approach to simulate the opening of the safety valve. Our recent work [23] proposed a coupled conjugate heat transfer and CFD model to capture the venting and jet fire behaviour during TR. Li et al. [36] constructed a three-dimensional (3-D) high-resolution model for venting of LIBs, and the ensemble-averaged flow field through caps was investigated. Vendra et al. [37] employed the experimentally measured temperature and pressure data as boundary conditions to simulate TR-induced fire. However, the transport of the ejected particles was not considered in any of the above models, since the traditional CFD models face difficulty in resolving such particles with a small size scale of millimetres and microns [23,35].

Existing LIB models are therefore not yet capable of providing full answers to the multi-scale challenges covering all phenomenon during TR. A TR model incorporating the multi-phase process of fluid flow and particle motion outside the batteries is of utmost importance, which can be considered the building blocks of a comprehensive model for fire propagation in modules and packs.

In the present study, an advanced multi-scale and multiphase modelling framework is proposed to consider the major abuse chemical reactions and physical transformation within batteries, from the initial generation of heat and gases to the eventual deposition of particles. The model is initially established for a prismatic cell and implemented within the frame of open source CFD code OpenFOAM. In addition, one TR experiment is conducted in the combustion chamber to visualize the jet flow and obtain the characteristic parameters. At the same time, another is undertaken in a self-made sealed chamber to collect the settleable particles and acquire their size distribution as the model inputs. The model is validated quantitatively with measurements of cell temperature, jet velocity and mass loss evolution and qualitatively by the predicted and observed temporal evolution of plume morphology. The novelty and contribution of the model can be summarized as follows.
•
A multi-scale modelling framework was firstly developed to address the multiphase issue of LIBs during TR.
•
The interaction of various chemical reactions and physical transformation inside batteries and fluid flow and particle motion outside batteries have been captured.
•
The complete evolution of the ejected particles from generation, jet, spread to final deposition has been simulated.

The overall workflow of our research is given in Fig. 2 , and this paper is organized as follows: Section 2 initially discusses the venting mechanism and describes the model framework according to three levels. Section 3 introduces the experimental design and model setup. Section 4 presents the simulation results of the venting behaviour of LIBs in chronological order of the TR process. Finally, Section 5 concludes the paper with some key findings and future work.

The jet behaviour of LIB cells during TR is the result of the combined action of chemical reactions and physical transformation, involving the generation and conversion process among solid, liquid and gas phases. The schematic in Fig. 3 illustrates the transition process and mechanism for various stages during TR, making an answer to questions about why the jet flow is accompanied by emissions of droplets and particles during the venting of LIBs and what transformation the battery materials experience under thermal abuse. As shown in Fig. 3, there are three regions, i.e. jelly roll (region 1), phase interface and headspace (region 2) and ambient fluid (region 3), corresponding to diverse mechanisms.

Region 1: When approaching onset temperatures, a chain of electrochemical reactions take place within the jelly roll of the cell, generating massive heat and gases [38,39]. Numerous gas pockets will be formed between electrode layers due to rapid gas generation [40]. The electrode materials will be fractured by the consequent pressure difference between layers due to the inhomogeneity of cell temperature and gas generation [40]. Meanwhile, the solid materials, including the separator, the collector and some carbonate, will melt under such an elevated temperature. The graphite will remain solid considering the high melting point and boiling point [19]. However, owing to the degradation of the binder, the weakening constraint imposed by adhesion might lead to inter-particle fracture and pulverization.

Region 2: Evaporation and entrainment are the major transformation processes occurring at the interface and headspace within the battery. With the increase of cell temperature, the organic solvents are vaporized to approach their saturation pressure, which together with the generated gases lead to pressure accumulation in headspace [30]. Once the pressure reaches its designed critical value, the safety valve will work and release gases. In addition to gas emissions, the bulk fluid of electrolyte and solid particles are carried along with the gas flow and arrive at the safety valve through headspace, known as the entrainment phenomenon. The entrainment mechanism of electrolyte bulk fluid is attributed to the detachment of liquid under the shearing force of gases [41] while the particles are entrained due to the momentum exchange between gas and solid.

Region 3: As the gases with high momentum are fed into a quiescent environment, a jet flow will be formed and carry the particles moving together. The entrained bulk fluid of electrolyte will be fragmented and atomized into smaller droplets on the effect of aerodynamic forces. The electrolyte vapours will also be liquefied into droplets when encountering the ambient fluid with low pressure and temperature. Once in contact with the air, some oxidizable substances in the particles will react with oxygen and cool down [20]. The carbonate, separator and meatal collector in the molten state are reconverted to solid under cooling by the ambient fluid [19].

Based on the above analysis of the venting process, a numerical model was developed and implemented in OpenFOAM to capture the underlying phase transition and dynamics of the particle laden gas jet. The flow chart of the TR model is shown in Fig. 4 , consisting of several simulation steps: (1) solving the heat and gas generation issue within the jelly roll, as to be described in Section 2.2; (2) predicting the pressure accumulation employing the lumped model in battery-scale according to the conservation of battery materials at phase interface and headspace, and then calculating jet kinetic parameters in Section 2.3; (3) utilizing kinetic parameters both from calculation and experiments as dynamic boundary conditions; (4) resolving the Eulerian field in room-scale to simulate the fluid flow evolution in Section 2.4.1; (5) resolving the Lagrangian field in particle-scale to model the jet and dispersion of particles in Section 2.4.2; (8) repeating the above steps until the end time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modelling in jelly roll: heat and gas generation

ce:section.ce:para:
A series of exothermic and electrochemical reactions will appear for the LIB cells under thermal abuse, resulting in continued temperature rise [38,39]. The first exothermic reaction is the decomposition of the solid electrolyte interface (SEI) layer, which is formed on the graphite anode due to the irreversible electrochemical [42]. Then the anode will react with the electrolyte when the temperature reaches around 120 °C [43]. In addition, the exothermic decomposition of cathode materials will also occur and release oxygen. When the cell temperature exceeds about 200 °C, the electrolyte will also decompose exothermically itself [44]. Finally, the binder used to maintain the electrodes' integrity will react with the cathode material and LixC6 [38,39]. The above processes could be described by a lumped model incorporating the conservation of energy, which is expressed by:

In addition to heat, the thermal abuse reactions also generate gases including carbon monoxide, carbon dioxide, hydrogen and some hydrocarbon [9,10,47,48], which contribute to the partial pressure in the cell. Initially, the breakdown of the SEI layer results in the release of carbon dioxide and ethylene [38,43]. Then the carbon dioxide might be reduced with intercalated lithium at the anode, which is responsible for the release of carbon monoxide [49–51]. Additionally, lithium also reacts with the solvent, generating some methane and ethane [52–54]. The decomposition of the cathode is one possible source of oxygen [55], but its contribution to pressure accumulation was not considered for its consumption in electrolyte reduction. The oxidation of the electrolyte solvent is another possible generation process of carbon dioxide [38]. Eventually, the reactions between polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) and Li exposed in the surrounding electrolyte will occur and cause hydrogen generation [50]. Considering the complexity of the reaction mechanism within the cell, it is assumed that the generation mechanism of each gas from one reaction stage corresponds to a primary chemical reaction to obtain an able-decoupled relationship between the generation of gas component and reaction degree. The reaction mechanism and corresponding chemical equations considered in this work are summarized in Table 1 .

Table 1 shows the correspondence between gas species and TR reactions, but the quantitative relationships cannot be determined since one gas component could come from multiple sources. To quantitatively describe the contributions of each abuse reaction in the generation of each gas component, a matrix ω

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modelling at phase interface and headspace: evaporation, venting and entrainment

ce:section.ce:para:
The porous space of the separator and electrodes in the jelly roll is filled with the liquid electrolyte, which generally consists of organic solvents with higher volatility. As shown in Fig. 3, the electrolyte with the initial state of liquid will be transformed into gases under thermal abuse, and two sources determine its mass loss:

During TR, the pressure inside the cell will accumulate due to gas generation and electrolyte evaporation. When the internal pressure of the LIB cell increases to a critical pressure, the vent valve will open and release the generated gases with the particles carried along. The dynamic change of cell internal pressure at any time is calculated by a unified differential form of state equation of ideal gas from our previous work [23]:

C d is a discharge coefficient considering a non-ideal effect of the irreversible process [60], while φ is a fitting coefficient less than 1 viewing the blocking of ejected solid materials to the safety valve, with the value of 0.12. The gas flow velocity u can be calculated using a variant of the isentropic nozzle flow equations [61]:

Considering the under-expanded jet flow will expand into the supersonic flow after leaving the nozzle, the state parameters of the steady flow should be obtained to provide an equivalent inlet condition for subsequent numerical simulation. where C
D is a volume discharge coefficient considering a possible non-uniform velocity profile at the nozzle with the value of 0.6 [64].

In addition to the electrolyte vapours and the generated gases, the solid particle is also a major emission component from TR batteries. A schematic diagram according to the X-ray imaging of the TR cell reported by Finegan et al. [40,65] is illustrated in Fig. 5 to explain how the fragments of electrode materials leave the jelly roll and then are ejected in the form of particles. As shown in Fig. 5, the whole process can be divided into four stages.
Stage I: The battery is in the initial state, and the jelly roll and the safety valve remain intact.
Stage II: Thermal degradation reactions release massive gases as the cell temperature rises. The consequent pressure difference between layers will fracture the electrode materials due to the inhomogeneity of cell temperature and gas generation rates [40]. The binder also begins to decompose when the cell approaches the onset temperature of its degradation reaction, causing the electrode particle attached to the binder debonding. The above two effects might be responsible for inter-particle fracture and pulverization. In addition, the rapid gas generation also results in the formation of gas pockets between the electrode layers, and they will be channelled towards the headspace driven by the pressure gradient [40].
Stage III: The fragmented electrode materials are with the motion of the gas pockets [65]. When the gas pockets approach the interface, some particles can be transported and projected from the interior of the jelly roll to the headspace. Some particles near the gas pockets will also move upward via channels dragged by the gas flow until they are ejected into the free space [40]. When the projected particle arrives near the safety valve, it will be accelerated due to the increasing momentum at the nozzle and then ejected into the ambient environment.
Stage IV: The ejection of particles leaves numerous channels of gas pockets, which explains the collapse in the structure of the spiral-wound layers in the X-ray images [40,65].

The above mechanisms indicate that the high-speed spouting gases carry the solid particles during the cell venting. Therefore their mass flowrate can be expressed as the function of the velocity of the carrier gases [66]:
(15)
m ˙

where A
v is regarded as the area of the safety valve, a and b represent the calibration coefficients of the mass rates. Since the adhesion from the residual binder might offset the partial drag force applied to the solid particles and impact the entrainment rates, it is hard to find an exact principle to describe this process. Therefore, the mass flowrate of the solid particles needs to be calibrated according to the measurement. Because it faces challenges in measuring the flowing rates of the ejected solid particles directly, the mass loss for solid is assumed to be proportional to the total mass loss. The transient mass evolution for the ejected solid particles could be defined as follow:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modelling in ambience: gas flow and particle transport
ce:section.ce:para: After the vent valve is broken, the pressurized gas will jet along with the particles, which is a rather complex and transient process involving fluid flow, particle transport and heat transfer. There are two methods for investigating the spread and deposition of the gaseous-solid phases: the Euler-Euler approach and the Euler-Lagrange approach. The Euler method regards both the gas flow and dispersed phase as interpenetrating continua, while the Lagrange method emphasises the individual behaviour of each particle. In our work, the Discrete Phase Model coupled with the Computational fluid dynamics (DPM-CFD) based on the Euler-Lagrange frame was employed to simulate the venting process of LIBs, where the fluid phase is modelled as a continuum while particles are treated as a discrete phase. In the DPM, each trajectory is computed for a parcel representing a group of particles of equal sizes, which could save considerable computational effort.

The compressible Navier–Stokes equations combined with an energy conservation equation were employed to compute the mass, momentum, energy, and species conservations for the continuous phase outside the LIBs. The turbulence state of the jet flow of venting gases was solved utilizing Reynolds-averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) equations, where the standard k–ε model was selected considering its outstanding performance in predicting turbulent shear flows. The conservation equations for the gaseous phase are shown as follows:

After being ejected from the safety valve, the particles will be driven owing to the energy exchange from gas to solid and carried by the jet flow. Meanwhile, some reactions, such as the oxidation of particles, will occur, leading to the mass loss of the discrete phase. However, the oxidation mechanism and transformation rates at different size ranges of particles have not been clear yet, so the mass conversion for particles was neglected in the present simulation. During the jet, diffusion and sedimentation process of ejected particles, the balance of various forces acting on a single particle is based upon the prediction for the trajectory of the particle, which is solved by applying Newton's second law to a particle, rendering the following force balance:

The term F denotes the additional forces on the ejected particles per unit mass, generally including the pressure gradient force, virtual mass force, Saffman lift force, and Magnus lift force. In this work, since the particle shape was assumed as a simplified sphere and the particle rotation was neglected, the pressure gradient force and the Magnus lift force were not considered rather only the drag force, the gravity, the buoyancy, and the Saffman lift were retained in the present simulation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristic parameters of thermal runaway and visualization of the jet
ce:section.ce:para: The external heating abuse tests have been conducted in the combustion chamber to aim at obtaining the high spatiotemporal resolution images of the jet flow field and some characteristic parameters during TR to validate the developed novel models. The battery used for the test was a prismatic LiFePO4 battery with a size of 148 mm in length, 90 mm in height and 27 mm in width. The total mass of the cell is measured as 625 g. The total electrolyte mass is estimated as 87.5 g [70]. Its nominal capacity and voltage are 27 Ah and 3.2 V. Cells were pre-cycled using a cycler (Neware) at a 0.1 C current rate within 2.65–3.65 V for three cycles and then followed by a constant current charging to achieve 100% SOC.

The schematic of the experimental devices for the visualization of jet flow and acquisition of characteristic parameters of TR is illustrated in Fig. 6 (a). The battery was fixed in a specially designed metal fixture and exposed to the open air in a combustion chamber. An electric heater with a power of 2000 W and size of 130 mm × 150 mm fixed beside the battery was employed to trigger the battery falling into TR. A thermal insulting layer was placed between the heating plate and the fixture to reduce heat dissipation. The 0.5 mm K-type chromel-alumel thermocouples with 0.5 s response time and ±1.0 °C accuracy were employed to obtain the temperature data, which was attached to the cell's surface away from the heater. A venting tunnel with the same size as the safety valve was placed on the top of the battery to rectify the gas jet during TR, and the pitot tube was used to measure the jet velocity through the venting tunnel. The measurement devices were placed on the electronic balance to record the mass data of the battery. In this work, the planar laser scattering (PLS) technique was applied to observe the jet flow relying on the diffuse reflection of ejected particles and droplets during the venting event. But it should be noted that the rectification device and the pitot tube were removed in the experiment of jet visualization to avoid their influence on the morphology of jet flow. The PLS system consisted of a continuous-wave sheet laser with a wavelength of 520 nm and power of 1.0 W, a mirror to reflect laser light in a narrow space and a high-definition camera with an exposure time of 1/60 s. The camera recording at 50 frames per second was placed about 1.5 m in front of the battery to capture the evolution of jet flow during TR. Once the TR occurred, the heater power was cut off immediately.

The method of coupling the Pitot tube and balance measurement was employed to obtain a more accurate gas venting velocity [71,72]. Considering the process of gas venting is overweight for mass balance, the force analysis should be conducted on the interface between the battery measurement system and the mass balance to eliminate the impact of counter force, as shown in Fig. 6 (a).
(26)

As illustrated in Fig. 6 (b), another TR experiment was conducted in a self-made sealed chamber to collect the settleable particles ejected from the failure battery and acquire the size distribution as the model inputs. The heater with the same specifications was used for triggering TR. The sealing cap at the top would be opened after TR to release the generated gases and evaporate the ejected liquid electrolyte. The particles deposited on the battery and heater surface and attached to the chamber surface were carefully collected after the cell cooled down and then divided into four samples (#1, #2, #3, #4) using 60-mesh, 20-mesh, and 10-mesh sieves, with corresponding sieve mesh diameters of 0.3 mm, 0.9 mm, and 2.0 mm, the physical appearance of which is shown in Fig. 7 (a). The mass of the collection particle was 42.63 g measured by a million-point electronic balance, accounting for approximately 5.6% of the total battery mass. The mass of samples #1, #2, #3 and #4 were detected as 23.42 g, 11.22 g, 2.21 g and 5.20 g, making up 54.9%, 26.3%, 5.2% and 12.2% of the ejected particles.

The surface morphology of the particle sample was observed using the scanning electron microscope (a1-a6) and optical microscope (a7-a8), as shown in Fig. 7 (a). An analysis of sample #1 in Figure a1 and a2 revealed that raw particles were approximately spherical and irregularly spheroid with nonuniform size, and the particle surface was rough. For samples #2 and #3, it was found that multiple raw particles agglomerated into larger particles with an irregular shape, as presented in a4-a6. In addition, the observation of sample #4 using an optical microscope indicated the particles larger than 2.0 mm in diameter were mainly composed of two battery materials: one is considered as the separator that melted and solidified again, and the other is the fragments of metal collector, as shown in a7 and a8, respectively. In this work, the particles were considered to be regularly spherical to simplify the computation process.

The particle size distribution of the samples was characterized using a laser particle size analyser, as shown in Fig. 7 (b). Two size peaks were found that the samples reached the 1st maximal percentage at about 7.5 μm of the equivalent particle size while the 2nd peak was at 79 μm. It should be noted the samples were not ground but instead remained in their original state. Thus, the 1st peak of particle size was considered the size distribution for the raw graphite particles, while the 2nd peak corresponded to the agglomerated particles under the effect of the binder. The d 10 value in ejected particle samples was 13 μm, indicating particles with an equivalent particle diameter of less than 13 μm account for 10% of the total particles. Similarly, d 50 and d 90 were 141 μm and 349 μm, respectively.

In order to obtain the input of the discrete phase for the model, the size distribution functions of ejected particles should be fitted, where the two-parameter distribution functions are commonly used. According to the test results in Fig. 7 (b), the particle size distribution of smoke particles should follow the normal logarithmic function and the differential distribution function is expressed as:
(31)
f
( d )
=
1
2 π
σ d

)

)

where d
m is the median diameter and σ is the standard deviation. In this work, two log-normal curves are employed to fit the size distribution of particle samples for the consideration of double peaks and the fitting results are drawn in Fig. 7 (b). The fitting and characteristic parameters of ejected particles are summarized in Table 2 .

A 3-D numerical model has been instantiated within OpenFOAM. For the actual application scenarios, several large-format batteries are generally installed into a semi-confined battery pack, indicating a more compact operating environment and massive release of particles and gases. For comparing the experimental results, the computational geometry followed the actual structure of the combustion chamber and the schematic as well as meshes are illustrated in Fig. 8 . The computational domain zone was modelled as the fluid space with the size of 2.0 m × 2.0 m × 2.0 m, initially filled with 79.05% nitrogen and 20.95% oxygen. Half of the computational grid comprised of hexahedron elements was generated depending on geometric symmetry to reduce the computational cost to resolve all the domain zone. It should be noted that the mesh must be larger than the diameter of the largest particle in the DPM to ensure calculation accuracy. Therefore, the maximum diameters of particles were limited to less than 0.5 mm, and the minimum size of the meshes for the fluid fields was 2.0 mm. In addition, a local slice of the fluid and particle fields near the battery is selected to be discussed in Section 4.3, as shown in Fig. 8, considering that the jet flow is a plane formed by the PLS technique. Section 4.4 focuses on particles' spread and deposition characteristics in the whole computational domain.

The boundary condition consists of five parts: the ground, the wall of the chamber, the outlet connected to the exhaust fan, the cell surface and the venting inlet. The boundary area of the venting inlet is regarded as the area of a total burst disk with the value of 162.3 mm2 since the burst disk has been entirely disengaged after the venting event. A series of dynamic boundary conditions where the critical parameters were calculated from the lumped model were applied to the cell surface and the venting inlet, and their detail simulation conditions are summarized in Table 3 .

Considering the sub-atmospheric pressure caused by the exhaust fan, the pressure outlet for the gaseous phase and the escape condition for the particle phase were prescribed at the boundary of the outlet. Finally, all the wall and ground regions were specified with the no-slip conditions, while the elastic collision for both normal and tangential directions was defined for the motion of particles in confined space boundary conditions.

When suffering from thermal abuse, the interior and exterior of the LIB cells will undergo a series of physical and chemical transformations. The overall evolution was recorded by the visualization technology of sheet laser coupled with a digital camera in our newly conducted experiment, as shown in Fig. 9 . According to the morphology of the jet flow and divergence of critical parameters, the whole thermal abuse process can be divided into five stages with several key features: initial heating stage, the opening of safety valve, transition stage, thermal runaway and cooling stage.

The plots in Fig. 10 (a) and (b) illustrate the dynamic evolution of the temperature and internal pressure within the battery and the jet flow velocity, respectively, providing deeper insights into the mechanism of the observed phenomenon in the experiment. In the early heating stage, no obvious external phenomenon was observed visually while the cell's internal temperature and pressure rose. As the cell temperature increased, the evaporation of electrolytes as well as the reaction gases contributed to the continuous improvement of internal cell pressure. The safety valve opening should be the sign of the 2nd stage, which is determined by the internal pressure of the cell and the operating pressure. According to Eq. (11), the magnitude of pressure increase inside the cell before venting is determined by two aspects: the temperature rise rate dT/dt and the gas generation rate dn/dt. The temperature rise rate is affected by the heat release rate, the external heating power and the heat dissipation. The generation rate for reaction gases is determined by the abuse reaction rate and corresponding gas quantity, while the cell temperature dominates the evaporation rate for the electrolyte. When the value reaches the critical pressure of 1.45 × 106 Pa [73], the vent valve opens to release the generated gases and electrolyte vapour. Owing to the incredible speed of gas flow, reaching approximately 55.2 m·s−1, a jet flow with a large dispersion angle was observed, as shown in Fig. 9. The 2nd stage only lasted for a few seconds followed by the transition of gas flow from a reverse conical contour to the buoyant plume. During the 3rd stage, the flow velocity maintained slight fluctuation, varying from 1.10 m·s−1 to 6.39 m·s−1 at the safety valve. With the acceleration of chemical reactions and gas generation rates within the battery, the jet flow with conical contour appeared again, indicating the battery fell into the TR stage. The division of this stage is based on the battery's internal pressure and jet flow speed determined by reaction rates instead of the temperature curve. Three peak values were captured, which were predicted as 371.2 °C for the cell temperature, 2.69 × 105 Pa for the battery internal pressure and 13.95 m·s−1 for the flow velocity, respectively. The abatement and extinction of the smoke plume mark the end of the TR stage and the beginning of the cooling stage. In spite of the massive consumption of active materials during the TR stage, a buoyancy-driven plume could still be observed, which may be caused by smouldering inside the battery [16]. Under the influence of thermal convection and radiation, the cell temperature gradually slowed down and approached the environment.

Fig. 10 also compares the cell temperature and speed of jet flow between the simulation and measurements. As shown in Fig. 10 (a), the simulated temperature evolution curve is in reasonably good agreement with the experiments, especially since the developed model well captured the peak temperature with a relative error below 1.0%. The comparison in Fig. 10 (b) also demonstrates the consistency of simulation and experiment in jet flow speed for various stages. The jet velocity at the safety valve was measured as 55.21 m·s−1 at the moment of venting and the peak value when the cell went into TR was 14.50 m·s−1, while the corresponding predicted velocities were 46.20 m·s−1 and 13.19 m·s−1 at the same time node. The relative errors are 16.3% and 9.03%, respectively. However, a marginal deviation existed in that the jet flow velocity fluctuated slightly during the transition stage in the experiment but not in the simulation. The propagation of TR inside the thin layer structure of the jelly roll will lead to an unstable abuse reaction rate, which may be responsible for such a fluctuation of speed in the experiment. However, the internal propagation was not considered in the TR-lumped model. Thus, a smooth curve was finally resolved in the simulation. Overall, the model is capable of simulating the venting processes of LIBs with reasonable accuracy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conservation and conversion of various components
ce:section.ce:para: The mass loss attributed to the ejection of battery materials is a significant characteristic of the TR process for LIBs, which involves the conservation and conversion of multiphase and multicomponent. The plots in Fig. 11 (a) illustrate the dynamic evolution of total mass loss during the whole TR process, where the mass of the battery was normalized with an initial value of zero. At the initial heating stage, no mass change was observed owing to the sealing of the safety valve, while an instant mass drop with the value of 14.78 g occurred at the moment of valve opening, indicating the ejection of abundant battery materials. It was found that the solids and electrolyte vapours occupied the major release of venting materials, which were calculated as 7.19 g and 3.15 g, respectively, for the primary ejection. Subsequently, the battery mass presented a persistent decline until the internal decomposition reactions ended, and the battery went into a cooling stage. Eventually, the total mass loss was simulated as 139.96 g, where the ejected electrolyte vapours, generated gases and solid particles contributed 70.69 g, 15.44 g and 53.83 g, respectively, accounting for 50.5%, 11.1% and 38.4% of the whole. In addition, the transient curves of overall mass loss from prediction and measurement by the electronic balance are also compared in Fig. 11 (a). It could be found that the calculated results of mass evolution are highly consistent with the ones obtained through experimental validation. The standard deviation of mass loss during the whole venting process is below 3% and the relative error of the total mass loss is lower than 1.0%, demonstrating that the model can predict the mass flowrate of battery materials during TR. But there is a sharp-pointed peak at 669 s of the 2nd stage for the experiment. The reaction force on the electronic balance caused by the high-speed jet flow might be responsible for such a significant rebound of mass curves in the experiment. Still, the influence of force was not considered in this thermal model. In addition, it should be worth noting that the evolution of cell mass loss generally presents uncertainty in different tests [74]. The other abuse conditions, such as the heating temperature [75], heating position [76] and the preload force [72], also have an impact on the mass loss of the cell. Walker et al. [74] analysed the total mass loss after TR using the large format fractional TR calorimeter and gas collection methodology, finding that the standard deviation of ejecta mass was roughly 4.66%. Zhou et al. [75] also reported that the mass loss standard deviation could reach 7% for the prismatic cell during repeated experiments. Considering that the present model is calibrated based on a single test, the input parameters used in this model should also possess a similar uncertainty. Further analysis for the gas phase is illustrated in Fig. 11 (b), displaying the mass loss caused by the evaporation electrolyte and reaction gas components. It could be found that the electrolyte vapour dominated the mass variety of LIBs, accounting for 82.0% of mass loss for gas phase. The release mass for CO, CO2, H2 and alkane were calculated as 1.46 g, 10.73 g, 0.18 g and 2.11 g, respectively.

Fig. 11 (c) and (d) demonstrate the corresponding mass flowing rates for various phase states and gas species. As shown in Fig. 11 (c), two mass rate peaks were captured to release gaseous and solid phases: one peak at the time of vent valve opening and another at TR. The first violent jet occurred at approximately 667 s with a mass flowing rate of 1.66 g·s−1. The mass flowing rate only reached 0.77 g·s−1 when the cell underwent TR, but this process lasted longer. The progression of mass flow rates is shown in Fig. 11 (d). It was found that the electrolyte vapour, CO2 and C2H4 generated by the SEI layer decomposition are the principal materials at the opening of the safety valve, the value of which were calculated as 0.43 g·s−1, 0.10 g·s−1 and 0.06 g·s−1, respectively. However, owing to the depletion of electrolytes and the burst of chain reactions during the TR stage, the generated gases, including H2, CO, CO2 and hydrocarbon, became the major release of the gaseous phase.

Fig. 11 (e) displays the conversion process of an electrolyte by the mass evolution curves, giving crucial insight into what transformation the electrolyte undergoes during the TR process and what it eventually becomes. The conversion of electrolytes between liquid and vapours was the primary progression during the initial heating stage. Since the saturation pressure under VLE conditions increased with the cell temperature, the liquid electrolyte was continually evaporated to a gaseous state, leading to pressure accumulation within the battery headspace. The final evaporation mass was predicted as 5.38 g before venting, accounting for 5.95% of the whole electrolyte mass. The subsequent sharp decline of the vapours within headspace was due to the release after the safety valve opened, and then the electrolyte left the battery in the modality of vapours. From then on, the cell temperature reached about 208 °C, and the depletion from chemical reactions started at 877 s, attributed to the reactions between anode and electrolyte and its decomposition, and then increased steadily. Eventually, the raw liquid electrolyte was used up and converted into vapours and reaction gases. The corresponding consuming masses were 70.69 and 16.81 g, with mass fractions of 80.78% and 19.21%, respectively. In addition, it is worth noting that the electrolyte was almost wholly transformed before TR. Therefore, no electrolyte vapours are being ejected for the TR stage, which might enlighten the combustion simulation of LIBs. The previous literature [23,35,37] related to jet fire modelling did not consider the electrolyte as the species taking place in combustion, but no clear explanation for this assumption was given. The calculated results from this work prove that this hypothesis seems reasonable. Totally the simulation results reveal not only the mass loss of various ejected compositions but also provide a series of dynamic boundary conditions participating in the subsequent numerical simulation.

The evolution comparison between the jet flow visualized by the laser in the experiment and the distribution of ejected particles and gases obtained from the simulation are illustrated in Fig. 12 . The spatial diffusion of the jet and its mixing with particles determine the spray shape of the vertical section. At the moment of the safety valve opening, the particles and gases were ejected in the shape of an inverted cone. The diverging included-angles of the jet flow are determined by its turbulence intensity and the structure characteristics of the nozzle. The higher turbulence intensity indicates the jet flow is prone to mixing with the surrounding fluid, and the diverging included-angles are larger. The turbulence intensity generally increases with increasing velocity [77]. Therefore, owing to the larger velocity at the opening of the safety valve, the maximum diverging included-angles were observed to be 53.2° for prediction and 61.6° for the experiment at 668 s. With the rapid attenuation of the battery's internal pressure and the momentum, the diverging angles decreased until the effect of buoyancy governed the turbulent diffusion of jet flow, indicating that thermal abuse progression stepped into the transition stage. In this stage, the particles were driven by the upward buoyancy due to the fluid density gradient, forming a multiphase plume with a width of approximately 11 cm. Owing to the sharp rise of temperature and the massive generation of gas from thermal decomposition reactions during TR, the 2nd venting event occurred and resulted in another emergence of the reverse conical jet flow. Still, the lesser diverging included-angles were observed in comparison with the 1st venting due to smaller jet velocity in the TR stage. The maximal diverging included-angles was approximately 23.0° observed in the experiments, while the predicted value is 24.6° by the developed model, with a relative error of 6.5%. The simulated snapshots of the evolution of plume morphology and jet diverging angles are concluded to fit reasonably well with experimental results at various instants, proving reasonably good accuracy of this newly developed model to simulate the complicated process of battery TR and venting.

The ejection energy after venting is considered to include the kinetic energy and relative internal energy of both gases and particles, which can be estimated as:

where ṁ
total is the total mass flowrate of ejected materials, ṁ
i is the component. C
p,i is the heat capacity of each element of ejected materials. u and T are the velocity and temperature at the safety valve, and T 0 is the initial temperature of the cell. The first term represents the total kinetic energy of ejection while the second term is the total relative internal energy. The ejection energy is calculated as approximately 47.58 kJ. In addition, the amount of self-heating energy from TR can be estimated according to the conservation between the heat to increase cell temperature, the heat from external heating and the heat dissipation on the cell surface, calculated as approximately 268.91 kJ. The ejection energy accounts for about 17.69% of the total energy release of TR. One thing that should be noted that the venting gases can be easily ignited and result in the jet fire and/or even explosion, owing to containing abundant combustible components. Therefore, the combustion of ejected gases may become a new heat release source and bring a worse thermal hazard.

Fig. 13 (a) displays the temperature evolution of the particles and gas flow at various moments. A similar morphological evolution process of the temperature field to the particle cloud can be seen in the figure. Still, an increasing temperature trend was captured over time due to the growing battery temperature with the thermal abuse process. Furthermore, the radial profile of temperature and velocity field at the first venting and TR stage was also presented in Fig. 13 (b) and (c) to further elaborate the key characteristics and formation mechanism of the jet flow of LIBs. As the safety valve opens and a source of momentum is fed into a quiescent environment, the jet flow is generated. The velocity transverse gradient owing to the Kelvin–Helmholtz (K–H) instability on the jet boundary will result in the bifurcation of the jet flow into two regions known as the potential core region and the shear layer region, as marked in Fig. 13 (b) and (c). Certain vortex rings are formed from the shear layer following the K–H instability and entrain fluid from the ambience, leading to the jet spreading in the radial direction [78]. The above mechanism explains the formation of the reverse conical contour of LIB venting. In addition, the significant ring vortex regions can be observed in the velocity profile. The formation mechanism of recirculation might be attributable to the vortex driven by convective movement, owing to the temperature difference between the ejected gases and surrounding air. For the temperature field, it could be found the temperature was the highest near the central axis and gradually decreased along the radial direction at the various heights above the battery. The peak temperature at the centre also gradually increased as the heights improved. For example, the temperature reached 287 °C at 10 cm while it was predicted as 118 °C at 100 cm above the battery top during the TR stage, as shown in Fig. 13 (b). Additionally, the velocity field was found to present a similar characteristic to preserve the jet's momentum.

The previous section mainly concentrates on the jet flow characteristics in the near-battery region. Still, the ejected particles will also spread under the effect of the oncoming air and subsequently deposit on the influence of gravity in a larger-scale space. Since the ejected particles contain considerable inhalable particulate matters (d
p < 10 μm) and toxic elements, and the deposition particles may also damage electrical or electronic equipment, investigating the spreading behaviour of particles within a confined space is of necessity to personal safety and environmental protection, which aids to determining a proper ventilation rate to remove suspended particles. Fig. 14 (a) combines snapshots of the particle plume coloured by their sizes at different points in time, documenting its evolution of spread and deposition in the confined space. The plume was found to move along the vertical direction and diffuse to the plafond of the combustion chamber within 5 s. As the internal reactions occurred and battery pressure increased, the ejected particles continued to be generated and accumulated on the upper surface. Subsequently, the plume changed from an inverted cone shape to an umbrella shape at 50 s. It was noted that the particles with larger sizes began to sink firstly due to gravity, while the fine particles were dominated by buoyancy and maintained in suspension. Meanwhile, some particles were affected by the upward drag force of gas flow during the sedimentation process, changing their direction of movement. After venting for approximately 300 s, it was observed that the smoke almost filled the entire space of the chamber, and most was concentrated in the upper, which implies the need for top ventilation for the space. Even after 2000 s, most of the particles had been deposited on the ground, but there were still a few small particles with a diameter <10 μm suspended in the air, indicating the prolonged hazard of inhalable particulate matters after the LIB TR accident. Fig. 14 (b) displays the eventual distribution of deposition particles on the ground at various ventilation. Two outlines that the areas were covered by 90% of total particles (region 1) and 90% of particles with a diameter larger than 300 μm (region 2), respectively, were drafted in the figure. A preferential concentration of the larger dust particles in the near-battery region was found at the eventual distribution for various ventilations. From the comparison of deposition particle outlines at different ventilation rates, similar coverages were observed, with the area of 0.848 m2 and 0.218 m2 for region 1 and region 2, but the centre of the elliptical profile of region 2 gradually deviated from the battery pack with the increasing ventilation rates. The simulation results were consistent with the finding from Held et al. [21], demonstrating the ejected particles from LIBs will be transported over long distances and deposited on lower reaches of the surface with active ventilation.

The temporal mass evolution of the particles discharged from the exhaust fan, deposited on the ground and suspended in the air was plotted in Fig. 15 . The simulation plots capture the continuous upward trend for the discharged and deposited particles. At the same time, it could be found that the mass of suspended particles initially increased with the venting progression of LIBs and then decreased owing to natural settlement and ventilation. As shown in Fig. 15 (a), for the case without ventilation, the particle mass of the suspension state reached the peak of 39.1 g after 442 s of venting, which was also the end point of TR. Afterwards, because the total mass of particles was conserved, the mass of suspended particles started decreasing, and the deposition mass improved simultaneously, eventually obtaining about 53.8 g. It was noted that the rapid sedimentation of particles was concentrated in the early stage of the venting process, and the mass rate gradually attenuated over time. The diversity of sinking rates of particles of various sizes is responsible for this phenomenon that the larger particles will fall quickly to the ground after being ejected, contributing to the sharp mass variety in the early stage. With the increase of ventilation rates, the peak mass of particles suspending in the air was observed to reduce, which were 34.6 g, 21.8 g and 14.9 g for the flow rate of 100 L·h−1, 200 L·h−1 and 300 L·h−1, respectively. In addition, there was also a growing trend for the particles finally discharged from the exhaust fan as the ventilation rates were enhanced, which were 9.0 g, 13.3 g and 17.3 g for 100 L·h−1, 200 L·h−1 and 300 L·h−1, accounting for 17.5%, 24.9% and 32.0% of the total mass. Correspondingly, the ejected battery particles were effectively removed under improved ventilation conditions. The residual mass of deposition soot was calculated as 44.9 g, 40.8 g and 36.8 g for the air flow rates of 100, 200 and 300 L·h−1. In summary, the simulation results indicate that the ventilation conditions significantly affect the spread and deposition characteristics of ejected particles. A ventilation system is essential to capture the harmful particles after TR and prevent them from being released into the natural environment.

A multi-scale model for the multiphase venting process of LIBs has been developed to capture the transformation and the jet dynamic characteristics of gases and particles during TR. A lumped model in the battery-scale is employed to characterize the interplay of chemical reactions, phase change process (evaporation and entrainment) and pressure accumulation within the multiphase and multicomponent system of batteries. The jet flow of gases outside the cell is resolved by the CFD model at the room scale, while the ejection, spread and deposition of particles are addressed by the DPM at the particle scale. The dynamic boundary condition is employed to couple the TR process inside cells and the characteristics of the flow field outside cells. The following specific conclusions can also be summarized as follows.
(1)
Since the comprehensive mechanism of TR is covered, the multi-scale model can predict the transient evolutions of more characteristic parameters compared with the previous venting models, including the cell temperature, jet velocity and mass loss during TR, which make it suitable for capturing the complete multiphase process of TR and venting for LIBs.
(2)
The model reveals the respective contribution of battery materials in various phases to the mass evolution during the venting process. It is found that the electrolyte vapours dominate the gas release before TR, while the generated gases become the major release after the burst of chain reactions.
(3)
The predicted morphology evolution of particles and gases is qualitatively in line with the visualization by laser technique. Two violent jet flows with inverted conical contours were observed following the opening of the safety valve and TR. Also, the proposed model incorporates the spread and deposition process of the ejected particles and stresses the importance of ventilation to capture the ejected particles during TR.

This multi-scale model represents the integration of the entire chain of phenomena during TR from initial heat generation to final particle deposition, which can enhance the understanding of the venting phenomenon and guide the design of next-generation safe batteries. For the battery systems, the model is regarded as the first step towards predicting fire propagation with the ejected particles as ignition medium, which can serve as a meaningful analysing tool for the fire-proof design of the battery pack. Reducing the simplification of the TR mechanism and improving the universality of the TR model are the primary directions for further research. Since the present model was only validated based on a specific type of battery, the future work will extend it to other batteries with diﬀerent combinations of materials, venting parts and operating conditions, on the one hand. On the other hand, it is inspired to conduct further fundamental research on the transformation mechanism within the LIBs, including the quantitative description of the solid ejection and the relationship between the heat and gas generation, which aids in reducing the simplification in the present model and further improves its robustness.
